subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from a four-year university
Major was in Business Administration, with coursework in management and finance
Completed additional on-the-job training in office management and university-specific systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Title: Administrative Services Manager
Supports two Associate Vice Provosts (AVPs) in separate university offices under the Provost
Role encompasses office administration, budget management, event planning, and supervision of staff

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the Higher Education sector
Employed at a public university
Focuses on administrative support within academic and research programs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: University employs over 1,000 faculty and staff
Works in mid-sized administrative offices (5-15 staff per office)
Operates in a collaborative, academic environment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Public, non-profit university
Provides undergraduate, graduate, and research programs
Main offices serve both students and faculty across academic departments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has over 10 years of experience in administrative roles
Has served in the current university for more than a decade
Has seen multiple changes in university administration and processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered a senior-level administrator
Manages junior staff and student assistants
Reports directly to Associate Vice Provosts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Manages office communications, including answering phones and responding to emails
Oversees accounting for five offices and manages a grant budget
Plans and coordinates events, including logistics, catering, and facilities
Supervises two graduate student assistants
Maintains and updates the office’s social media accounts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 25% of time on event planning and coordination
Budgeting and accounting take up about 30% of work hours
Administrative support and communication (phones, emails) comprise 25% of daily activities
Social media management and outreach account for 10% of time
Remaining 10% is spent on ad hoc tasks from the AVPs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Exercises high autonomy in managing daily workflow and prioritizing tasks
Makes independent decisions on routine office matters and event logistics
Consults AVPs for high-level or policy-related decisions
Handles budget tracking and minor expenditures independently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Answering routine emails and phone inquiries
Updating calendars and scheduling meetings for AVPs
Coordinating logistics for small, recurring events

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Coordinating schedules for large group meetings with many stakeholders
Managing the budget and accounting for five distinct offices
Overseeing accreditation documentation and compliance processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Monthly gathering and input of invoices for budget ledgers
Routine email responses to frequently asked questions
Calendar management and meeting scheduling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys planning and executing events for graduate students
Finds student outreach and program promotion rewarding
Considers budget reconciliation and invoice entry to be tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Difficulty finding mutually available times for large group meetings
Time-consuming coordination between multiple offices for event logistics
Manual entry of budget data from disparate sources

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and engaged when working on event planning and student outreach
Skill level is highest in communications and office management tasks
Productivity declines on repetitive or highly manual data entry tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to trying new software that improves workflow
Motivated to learn tools that save time on repetitive tasks
Approaches new technology with cautious optimism

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts well to updates in familiar platforms like Google Suite and Microsoft Office
Requires hands-on learning or demonstrations for unfamiliar systems
Can quickly integrate new tools into daily routines after initial training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to attend short workshops or online tutorials outside peak busy periods
Prefers learning by doing rather than extensive theoretical courses
Will make time for tech training if the tool is directly relevant to job duties

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Heavy workload can make it difficult to carve out time for training
More likely to invest time if the technology addresses a current pain point
Sometimes hesitant if prior experiences with new tools have been frustrating

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Adoption is more likely if AVPs or colleagues endorse the tool
Regularly receives suggestions for new technology from peers in other offices
Management support increases willingness to experiment with new software

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google Suite (Gmail, Calendar, Drive) for communication and scheduling
Relies on Microsoft Office (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) for documentation and reporting
Utilizes Google Drive for collaborative document management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Desk phone system for voice communication
Social media platforms (e.g., Facebook, Twitter) for outreach
University’s internal accounting and HR portals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in Microsoft Office and Google Suite
Familiar with event management and room booking systems used in higher education
Experienced with basic content management systems for website updates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional formatting issues when transferring documents between Google and Microsoft platforms
Manual data entry required to reconcile accounting records from different office systems
Social media accounts are not fully integrated with internal calendars or communication tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI concepts such as chatbots, language models, and automation
Has encountered terms like 'machine learning', 'LLM', and 'natural language processing' in professional development workshops
Applies working knowledge of AI tools to streamline repetitive administrative tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and similar LLM-based tools for drafting communications and brainstorming ideas
Occasionally leverages AI-powered grammar and style checkers (e.g., Grammarly)
Has experimented with calendar assistant tools with AI-driven scheduling features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs weekly to draft responses to complex student or faculty emails
Employs AI tools to generate initial content for social media posts and event promotions
Utilizes AI-based calendar tools to propose meeting times across busy schedules

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used an LLM to quickly draft an event invitation that required only minor edits
AI-generated scheduling suggestions occasionally overlooked key participants’ constraints
Learned that AI-generated content still needs careful review to ensure accuracy and tone

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: University offers periodic workshops on emerging technology, including AI tools
Peers occasionally share tips and best practices for using LLMs in administrative tasks
Most AI learning has been self-directed or through informal peer support

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers to use AI tools independently for drafting and initial research
Occasionally interacts with AI tools iteratively for more complex tasks
Often reviews AI outputs in collaboration with colleagues before finalizing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for routine or time-consuming tasks
Occasionally relies on AI as an advisor for brainstorming or generating ideas
Does not delegate final decision-making to AI; maintains human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating AI-driven workflows if they clearly improve efficiency
Willing to test new AI features, especially for scheduling and document drafting
Prefers gradual adoption with clear training and support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces (like chatbots) more intuitive for drafting and brainstorming
Uses command-based interfaces for structured tasks, such as scheduling
Appreciates flexibility to switch between interaction styles as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks require understanding of university policies and procedures
Handling sensitive student or faculty issues relies on judgment and experience
AI is helpful for routine work but less suited for nuanced or confidential matters

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated drafts for routine emails or document templates
Always reviews AI outputs for accuracy and appropriateness before use
Does not rely on AI for high-stakes decisions or policy interpretation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive, time-consuming tasks (e.g., scheduling, draft writing)
Human oversight is essential for tasks involving discretion or sensitive information
Sees value in AI-assisted workflows that allow for easy human intervention

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for routine, low-risk processes like calendar suggestions
Feels automation is threatening if it bypasses necessary checks or context-specific judgment
Prefers clear options to override or edit AI-generated outputs

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances efficiency but does not replace the need for interpersonal and organizational skills
Feels communication and critical thinking are more important than ever alongside AI tools
Sees AI as complementing, not diminishing, core administrative competencies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Has learned to evaluate and edit AI-generated content for quality control
Developed skills in integrating AI tools into existing workflows
Occasionally responsible for advising colleagues on effective use of AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about AI replacing the overall role due to need for human judgment
Recognizes that some repetitive, manual tasks may be automated in future
Appreciates that role is evolving to include oversight of AI-enabled processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI-generated communications before sending
Maintains manual tracking for critical budget and compliance activities
Prefers systems that allow easy correction or customization of AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: University is cautiously exploring AI adoption with pilot projects and optional tools
Policies encourage human review and accountability for all AI-assisted tasks
Team leaders are supportive but prioritize training and risk mitigation

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI can increase efficiency and streamline administrative work in higher education
Acknowledges potential risks such as job displacement in highly repetitive roles
Optimistic that AI will free up time for more meaningful, student-focused work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports ethical guidelines for transparency and fairness in AI use
Concerned about data privacy when using AI tools with sensitive information
Believes human oversight is essential to prevent unintended consequences

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from smarter, context-aware scheduling assistants
Desires AI tools that can automatically reconcile budget data from multiple sources
Wishes for AI that can draft customized outreach messages based on previous communications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects increased automation of routine administrative tasks
Foresees greater emphasis on managing and overseeing AI-driven processes
Anticipates a shift toward more strategic, people-focused responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Advocates for ongoing training and support for staff adopting AI tools
Recommends clear policies on data privacy and oversight of AI systems
Suggests piloting new AI tools in low-risk areas before wider rollout
